steps.title: Identify the structure on each side
steps.content: Left: "There, wildlife" — the start of the subject noun phrase. Right: "is on the front lines of conservation efforts to bring adjutants back from near extinction" — an independent clause predicate. The blank must supply a title + name to complete the subject.

steps.title: Apply the rule: a title used directly before a proper name takes no commas
steps.content:
When a professional title functions as part of a name (no article like the), it runs directly into the name without punctuation.
  • Choice A — ‘biologist, Dr. Purnima Devi Barman,’: "wildlife biologist, Dr. Purnima Devi Barman, is..." — two commas around the name treat it as a non-restrictive appositive, but the sentence needs this name as the subject, not as added information about someone already identified. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘biologist: Dr. Purnima Devi Barman’: "wildlife biologist: Dr. Purnima Devi Barman is..." — a colon cannot appear in the middle of a subject noun phrase. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘biologist Dr. Purnima Devi Barman’: "wildlife biologist Dr. Purnima Devi Barman is on the front lines..." — title and name join directly as the full subject, no punctuation needed. ✓
  • Choice D — ‘biologist, Dr. Purnima Devi Barman’: "wildlife biologist, Dr. Purnima Devi Barman is..." — the opening comma suggests an appositive but there is no closing comma, making the structure unbalanced and the subject ambiguous. ✗
That confirms C is correct.
